config.title: Do You Need a Visa to Visit Berlin? FAQ
config.subtitle: A local's plain answers on entering Berlin in 2026: who needs a visa, the Schengen 90/180 rule, the new EES border checks, and where ETIAS stands.

items.q: Do you need a visa to visit Berlin?
items.a: It depends on your passport, travel document and status. EU, EEA and Swiss citizens do not need a visa for a short visit. Holders of common visa-exempt passports, including the US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan and South Korea, can normally visit for up to 90 days in any rolling 180-day period. Other travellers normally need a Schengen short-stay visa, but a residence permit, long-stay visa, EU-family status or special travel document can change the answer. Check the [German Federal Foreign Office visa table](https://www.auswaertiges-amt.de/en/visa-service/231148-231148) for your exact document.

items.q: How long can I stay in Berlin without a visa?
items.a: Visa-exempt visitors can normally stay up to 90 days within any rolling 180-day period. The limit is cumulative across the whole Schengen Area, and each day has its own 180-day look-back, so leaving for a few days does not reset it. Enter every previous arrival and departure in the checker, then verify a close plan with the [European Commission short-stay calculator](https://home-affairs.ec.europa.eu/policies/schengen/border-crossing/short-stay-calculator_en). If a short-stay visa sticker authorises fewer than 90 days, the sticker controls and the 90-day calculator does not extend it.

items.q: What is EES and do I need to do anything for it?
items.a: EES is the EU's Entry/Exit System and has been fully operational since 10 April 2026. It normally covers non-EU nationals making a short stay, whether visa-exempt or travelling with a short-stay visa, but exemptions include many residence-permit and long-stay-visa holders. Visa-free travellers normally have a facial image and four fingerprints stored; children under 12 are not fingerprinted. For short-stay visa holders, EES stores the facial image while fingerprints already collected for the visa remain in VIS. There is nothing to apply for or pay. Check the [official EES FAQ](https://travel-europe.europa.eu/ees/faq) for exceptions.

items.q: Do I need ETIAS to visit Berlin in 2026?
items.a: Not today. ETIAS is scheduled to start in the last quarter of 2026, and applications are not open yet. Launch will be followed by a transitional period of at least six months, when travellers should apply but will not be refused solely for lacking ETIAS if they meet the other entry conditions. A grace period of at least six months follows, when ETIAS is generally required but a limited first-entry exception applies. The future fee is 20 euros and an authorisation will normally last three years or until the passport expires. Follow the [official ETIAS page](https://travel-europe.europa.eu/en/etias), not a paid lookalike site.

items.q: Which countries need a Schengen visa for Germany?
items.a: Use the [German Federal Foreign Office visa table](https://www.auswaertiges-amt.de/en/visa-service/231148-231148), because the answer depends on the passport and sometimes on the travel document or residence status. If a Schengen short-stay visa is required and Germany is your main destination, apply through the responsible German mission with the requested documents. Apply well ahead, because appointment slots and processing times vary by country and season.

items.q: Does my passport need to be valid for a certain time?
items.a: Yes. For a Schengen trip your passport should be valid for at least three months beyond your planned departure date and have been issued within the last ten years. Checking this early is the single most common way to avoid a problem, so look at your expiry date before you book anything else.

items.q: Do EU, EEA or Swiss citizens need anything to visit Berlin?
items.a: For a short visit, EU, EEA and Swiss citizens do not need a visa or ETIAS and the Schengen 90/180 cap does not apply. Bring a valid passport or national ID card. EES does not apply to you. If you stay longer than three months, residence conditions and local registration can apply, so check the [official EU residence guidance](https://europa.eu/youreurope/citizens/residence/residence-rights/index_en.htm).
